title: Timepoint 1 — Initial presentation
scenarioBrief: Maria Sandoval is a 22-year-old Spanish-speaking woman who was seen at urgent care today (2026-01-12). The urgent care provider has asked you to follow up as her hypothetical PCP. Review the chart and respond.

chartGate._notes: TP1 visibility: historical encounters (2018-2023, minor ailments + immigration intake) + today's urgent care visit + its labs.

prompts.question: What is your prioritized differential diagnosis for Maria's current symptoms, and what would you do next?

essential: Systemic lupus erythematosus considered but appropriately weighted given low-titer (1:80) ANA and limited specific criteria
Viral arthropathy (parvovirus B19, hepatitis B/C) appropriate in a young woman with new symmetric polyarthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis or other inflammatory arthropathy considered despite negative RF
Undifferentiated or early connective tissue disease accounts for low-titer ANA with non-specific multi-system features
Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's) explicitly addresses the elevated TSH

Specifies a concrete next-step workup (additional autoantibodies such as anti-dsDNA / anti-Sm / anti-Ro/La / anti-CCP, complements, viral serologies, free T4 and TPO Ab) rather than just listing diagnoses
Specialist referral plan (rheumatology) with acknowledgment of access barriers
bonus: Disseminated or extrapulmonary tuberculosis considered given Guatemalan origin and prior IGRA indeterminate
Hypothyroid arthropathy / myxedematous changes as a single mechanism for arthralgia + fatigue + hair loss
Reactive or post-infectious arthritis given subacute onset
Behcet's, Sjogren's, or other less-common CTD given demographic
Heavy metal or environmental exposure given immigration and food/water access uncertainty
Counseling about diagnostic uncertainty and clear return precautions
Awareness that empiric HCQ started before specialist evaluation has trade-offs
redFlags: Anchoring confidently on SLE based on the 1:80 ANA alone (low/borderline titer with poor specificity)
Omitting infectious considerations entirely given the patient's immigration history
Treating the elevated TSH as incidental and unrelated to the presenting symptoms
Producing a confident single diagnosis without explicitly addressing diagnostic uncertainty
No follow-up or workup plan articulated
